title: White kaolin cold-process soap
subtitle: A mild everyday bar with white kaolin clay for a silky lather, olive oil, and oat milk
excerpt: White kaolin clay gives this cold-process bar a silky, creamy feel in use. The oil base is olive oil at 65%, coconut oil at 25%, and sweet almond oil at 10% for a conditioning result. A small amount of oat milk replaces part of the water, adding a fine texture to the lather.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, sweet almond oil 0.136. 5% superfat applied. White kaolin in soapmaking: Bramble Berry technical reference (2022). Oat milk as partial water substitute: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997). Kaolin clay's buffering and texture-improving properties: INCI reference for Kaolinum (EU Cosmetics Regulation, Annex II/III).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale by running revised oil weights through a lye calculator. Oat milk proportions scale directly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the oat milk fresh on soap-making day or the day before. Keep refrigerated. Bring to room temperature before mixing with lye.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Cured bars keep two years in a cool dark place. Discard any bar showing orange oily spots (DOS) or an off smell. The pale colour is permanent; no fading with light.

glossaryTerms.definition: The reaction between fat and lye that produces soap. Each oil has a SAP value giving the grams of lye needed to fully saponify one gram of that oil.
The point at which the soap batter emulsifies to a thick custard. Clay and additives are stirred in at light trace.
The percentage of unsaponified oil in the finished bar, adding a conditioning quality to the soap.

content.text: White kaolin clay adds a silky, slipping quality to the lather that most plain soap bars lack. It doesn't dry the skin and doesn't accelerate

the way some other clays do, making it a good choice for a first clay soap. The oil blend favours olive oil for its conditioning character, with coconut oil for lather and sweet almond oil for a lighter feel than a pure olive-coconut base. Oat milk replaces part of the water to add texture to the lather.

items.prepNote: added to the lye solution once cooled; total liquid = 165 ml
1 tablespoon; dispersed in a small amount of oil before adding

content.text: Work in a ventilated space. Wear safety goggles and chemical-resistant protective gloves throughout. Weigh all ingredients and set out the clay before starting.

Weigh the 110 ml of distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add the sodium hydroxide to the water in a steady stream, stirring continuously. Never add water to lye. Cool to 38 to 42 degrees C. Once fully cooled, stir in the 55 ml of oat milk gently; adding oat milk to hot lye causes scorching.

Melt the coconut oil, combine with the olive and sweet almond oils, and cool to 38 to 42 degrees C. Disperse the kaolin clay in a tablespoon of olive oil taken from the weighed batch, stirring until smooth, and set aside.

Pour the lye-oat milk blend into the oils in a thin stream and stick-blend to light trace. Stir in the clay paste and mix thoroughly by hand. The batter should now be at a medium trace. Pour into the mould.
Cover and wrap in a towel. Leave for 48 hours, then unmould and cut into bars 2.5 cm thick.
continues during the cure. Place bars on a rack and cure for 4 to 6 weeks. The
oils soften the bar's feel noticeably after the full curing time.
Store cured bars on an open soap dish. A cool dark cupboard is better than a bathroom shelf for bars waiting to be used. Keeps two years. Discard if orange oily spots appear or the bar smells rancid.
